meta.title: Angular
meta.description: Review checklist for Angular applications (standalone components, signals, RxJS)

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the change and any relevant context.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers without context waste time reverse-engineering intent from code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the code?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add more context about why this service was restructured?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves reviewer time.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each PR should do one thing. Mixing a feature with a refactor makes review harder.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large multi-concern PRs are slow to review and difficult to revert.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR touches multiple unrelated areas.
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds a new route and refactors the auth guard. Could we split these?

items.text: Are new components standalone rather than declared in NgModules?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Angular 14+ supports standalone components that do not require NgModule declarations. This is now the recommended approach.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Standalone components are simpler, reduce boilerplate, and enable better tree-shaking and lazy loading.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new components for standalone: true in the @Component decorator. Flag NgModule-declared components in standalone codebases.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new component is declared in a module. Since we are using standalone components, add standalone: true and import dependencies directly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use standalone components for all new work. They are simpler and the future of Angular.

items.text: Is there a clear separation between smart (container) and dumb (presentational) components?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Smart components handle data fetching and state. Dumb components receive data via @Input and emit events via @Output.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Dumb components are reusable, easy to test, and easy to reason about. Smart components centralize side effects.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if components both fetch data and render complex UI. Look for inject() or HTTP calls in presentational components.
baseContent.exampleComment: This list item component directly calls the API service. Consider passing data via @Input and emitting events for actions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep data fetching in smart components. Keep rendering in dumb components.

items.text: Are Angular signals used for local state where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Angular 16+ signals (signal(), computed(), effect()) provide fine-grained reactivity without RxJS overhead for synchronous state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Signals are simpler than BehaviorSubject for local component state and enable future zoneless change detection.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new BehaviorSubject usage for simple synchronous state that could be a signal instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: This BehaviorSubject holds a simple boolean toggle. A signal() would be simpler and does not require unsubscription.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use signals for local synchronous state. Use RxJS for async streams and complex event composition.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Component inputs and outputs should have explicit TypeScript types and use required where appropriate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untyped inputs default to any, bypassing TypeScript's safety and making templates error-prone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check @Input() and @Output() declarations for type annotations. Look for input() and output() signal-based APIs.
baseContent.exampleComment: This @Input() has no type annotation. Add the type to enable template type checking.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always type inputs and outputs. Use required: true for mandatory inputs.

items.text: Is OnPush change detection used for performance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Components with changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush only re-render when inputs change or events fire, not on every change detection cycle.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Default change detection checks every component on every cycle, which is wasteful for pure presentational components.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new components for changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush in the @Component decorator.
baseContent.exampleComment: This presentational component uses default change detection. Adding OnPush would improve performance.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use OnPush for all presentational components. It is easy to add and meaningfully improves performance.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Manual subscriptions must be unsubscribed on destroy. Use takeUntilDestroyed(), the async pipe, or toSignal() to automate this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked subscriptions cause memory leaks, phantom updates to unmounted components, and subtle bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for .subscribe() without corresponding unsubscribe logic. Check for missing takeUntilDestroyed().
baseContent.exampleComment: This subscription has no unsubscribe. Use takeUntilDestroyed() or the async pipe to auto-clean up.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never subscribe without a cleanup strategy. Prefer async pipe, toSignal(), or takeUntilDestroyed().

items.text: Are nested subscribes avoided in favor of RxJS operators?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Subscribing inside a subscribe callback is an anti-pattern. Use switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, or exhaustMap instead.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Nested subscribes create callback hell, miss error propagation, and make unsubscription complex.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for .subscribe() calls inside other .subscribe() callbacks.
baseContent.exampleComment: This has a nested subscribe. Use switchMap to flatten the observable chain.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Flatten observables with switchMap/mergeMap instead of nesting subscribes.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Observable chains should handle errors with catchError rather than letting them propagate and kill the stream.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An unhandled error in an observable kills the entire stream. For long-lived observables like store selectors, this means the UI stops updating.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check HTTP call chains for catchError or error callbacks. Verify retry logic where appropriate.
baseContent.exampleComment: This HTTP call has no error handling. Add catchError to show a user-friendly message and keep the stream alive.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always handle errors in observable chains. Unhandled errors kill the stream permanently.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Feature modules or standalone component routes should use loadComponent or loadChildren for code splitting.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Eager loading all routes in a large app creates a massive initial bundle that slows first paint.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check route configurations for static component imports vs dynamic loadComponent/loadChildren.
baseContent.exampleComment: This route eagerly imports a large feature component. Use loadComponent: () => import(...) for code splitting.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Lazy-load feature routes. Only eagerly load the shell and critical path.

items.text: Is the inject() function used instead of constructor injection where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Angular 14+ inject() function can replace constructor injection, especially useful in standalone components and functional guards.
baseContent.whyItMatters: inject() reduces boilerplate and works in functional contexts where constructor injection is not available.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for consistency. If the codebase uses inject(), new code should too. Not a hard rule.
baseContent.exampleComment: This guard uses a class with constructor injection. A functional guard with inject() would be simpler.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use inject() for consistency in codebases that have adopted it. Both patterns are valid.

items.text: Are routes protected with appropriate guards?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Routes requiring authentication or authorization should have canActivate guards that redirect unauthenticated users.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without guards, users can navigate directly to protected routes. Even if API calls fail, the page renders with broken state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new routes for canActivate/canMatch guards. Verify they redirect to login when unauthorized.
baseContent.exampleComment: This admin route has no auth guard. Add canActivate: [authGuard] to prevent unauthorized access.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Protect all routes that require authentication or specific roles with guards.

items.text: Are tests included for new or changed behavior?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New components, services, and pipes should have spec files. Bug fixes should include a regression test.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without tests, regressions go undetected until production. Tests document expected behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new .spec.ts files or additions to existing ones. Verify edge cases are covered.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new service has no tests. Could we add unit tests covering the success and error cases?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every behavioral change should have a corresponding test.

items.text: Is TestBed configured with minimal dependencies?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test modules should only import what the component under test needs. Importing entire application modules makes tests slow and brittle.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bloated TestBed configs slow down test suites and break when unrelated dependencies change.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check TestBed.configureTestingModule for unnecessary imports or declarations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test imports the entire SharedModule. Only import the specific components and services needed.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep TestBed lean. Only declare and import what the test actually needs.
